name: Inorder Traversal
summary: An inorder walk of a valid BST yields strictly increasing values; track the previous value and fail if the next one is not larger.
visualizationIntent: init: build and show the input tree from the level-order array; every node idle. inorder: the cursor descends to the leftmost node first, then visits the node, then its right child — the classic left-node-right order. Each visited node turns blue and its value becomes the new 'previous'. If a visited value is <= previous the node flips red (error): the inorder sequence stopped strictly increasing, so the tree is not a BST. done: the boolean answer; a valid BST is exactly the tree whose inorder visit order is strictly increasing.

lineExplanations.3: Count how many array slots describe the tree.
lineExplanations.4: Start an empty list of real node objects for the drawing.
lineExplanations.5: Start a lookup from a node's id to its object.
lineExplanations.6: Start a per-node state tracker: unseen, on-stack, done, or invalid.
lineExplanations.7: Loop over every slot to initialize its state.
lineExplanations.9: Point the child-slot walker just past the root.
lineExplanations.10: Loop over every slot to build the node objects.
lineExplanations.11: Only non-null slots become real nodes.
lineExplanations.12: Create this node with its value and two empty child links.
lineExplanations.13: Is there a slot left to be the left child?
lineExplanations.14: Is that candidate slot a real (non-null) node?
lineExplanations.15: Link that slot as this node's left child.
lineExplanations.16: Advance the walker past the left-child slot.
lineExplanations.17: Is there a slot left to be the right child?
lineExplanations.18: Is that candidate slot a real (non-null) node?
lineExplanations.19: Link that slot as this node's right child.
lineExplanations.20: Advance the walker past the right-child slot.
lineExplanations.21: Add the finished node to the tree.
lineExplanations.22: Record the node under its id for O(1) lookup.
lineExplanations.23: The root is slot 0, or nothing when the tree is empty.
lineExplanations.24: A one-slot holder for the previously visited value (starts empty).
lineExplanations.25: Walk the tree in order, checking the values increase.
lineExplanations.26: Return whether the inorder sequence was strictly increasing.
lineExplanations.29: Base case: an absent child is trivially valid.
lineExplanations.30: Return True for a missing node.
lineExplanations.31: Mark this node active — its frame is on the call stack.
lineExplanations.32: First, recurse all the way into the left subtree.
lineExplanations.33: Did the left subtree already break the order?
lineExplanations.34: If so, stop and return False.
lineExplanations.35: Read this node's value — visited after its whole left subtree.
lineExplanations.36: Recall the value of the previously visited node.
lineExplanations.37: Is there a previous value to compare against?
lineExplanations.38: Is this value not larger than the previous one?
lineExplanations.39: Order broken — mark this node invalid (red).
lineExplanations.41: Record this value as the new previous.
lineExplanations.42: This node passed — mark it visited.
lineExplanations.43: Now recurse into the right subtree.
lineExplanations.44: Return whether the right subtree stayed in order.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: the wrapper class LeetCode expects.
syntaxExplanations.2: def isValidBST(self, values): the entry method; values is the tree as a level-order array.
syntaxExplanations.3: len(values) counts the elements in the input array.
syntaxExplanations.5: by_id = {} creates an empty dictionary (a map from id to node).
syntaxExplanations.6: state_by_id = [] will hold one status number per node id.
syntaxExplanations.7: for i in range(n): loops i from 0 to n-1.
syntaxExplanations.8: append(0) adds a 0 (unseen) entry for this slot.
syntaxExplanations.9: child = 1 sets the next-child pointer to slot 1.
syntaxExplanations.10: for i in range(n): a second pass, this time to build nodes.
syntaxExplanations.11: is not None checks the slot holds a real value.
syntaxExplanations.12: A dict literal { ... } builds this node object.
syntaxExplanations.13: if child < n: is there still a slot available?
syntaxExplanations.14: Check the candidate child slot is not null.
syntaxExplanations.15: node["left"] = child stores the left child's id.
syntaxExplanations.16: child = child + 1 advances to the next slot.
syntaxExplanations.17: if child < n: is there another slot for the right child?
syntaxExplanations.18: Check the candidate right slot is not null.
syntaxExplanations.19: node["right"] = child stores the right child's id.
syntaxExplanations.21: nodes.append(node) adds the built node to the list.
syntaxExplanations.22: by_id[i] = node registers the node under its id.
syntaxExplanations.23: A ? B : C-style conditional: 0 if there is a root, else None.
syntaxExplanations.24: prev = [None] is a one-element list used as a mutable holder.
syntaxExplanations.25: self.inorder(...) starts the inorder traversal at the root.
syntaxExplanations.26: return answer hands back the True/False verdict.
syntaxExplanations.28: def inorder(self, node_id, prev, by_id, state_by_id): the recursive inorder checker.
syntaxExplanations.29: if node_id is None: the base case — an absent child.
syntaxExplanations.30: return True: an empty subtree is in order.
syntaxExplanations.32: self.inorder(...left...) visits the left subtree first.
syntaxExplanations.33: left_valid == False checks whether the left side failed.
syntaxExplanations.34: return False propagates the failure up.
syntaxExplanations.36: prev[0] reads the previously visited value out of the holder.
syntaxExplanations.37: if previous is not None: skip the check for the very first node.
syntaxExplanations.38: value <= previous: strictly increasing is required (<= fails).
syntaxExplanations.40: return False: the order was broken.
syntaxExplanations.41: prev[0] = value stores this value as the new previous.
syntaxExplanations.44: return right_valid ends this call with the right subtree's verdict.
